critic_raw:
The design applies a stratified random split to 5-year daily stock price time series for a transformer vs. ARIMA comparison. This is the most severe instance of temporal evaluation mismatch in this set. Stock prices are the canonical example of a non-stationary process with strong temporal autocorrelation — the 'assumed cross-sectional patterns over time' defense is inverted for financial data. A randomly shuffled split means the model is trained on, say, 2022 and 2024 data and tested on 2021 data, inverting causality. The ARIMA model, which is explicitly a temporal model, cannot function correctly on a shuffled input (ARIMA assumes consecutive observations) and would have to be refit on training windows that skip across the time series — this is likely not how the ARIMA is actually implemented, meaning the comparison is between a transformer trained on shuffled data and an ARIMA that must be handled differently.

The transformer for stock price prediction with lagged prices and technical indicators as features is extremely susceptible to look-ahead bias. Technical indicators like moving averages, RSI, and MACD computed on the full time series before splitting will include future price information in their calculation windows — a 200-day moving average computed on the full 5-year series and then split randomly contains future prices in what appears to be a 'historical' indicator at any given timestep.

MAE as the primary metric for stock price prediction measures absolute error in price units. Without normalizing by price level, MAE on a $100 stock vs. a $2,000 stock is not comparable — a $5 MAE is 5% error on the first but 0.25% on the second. For a multi-stock evaluation, this aggregation is meaningless. The appropriate metric is mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy (fraction of correct movement predictions), which is what trading decisions actually require.

The ARIMA models are 'tuned explicitly for each stock' while the transformer is presumably a single model. This is an asymmetric comparison: the ARIMA is a per-stock specialized model, while the transformer is evaluated as a general model — the comparison tests single-stock ARIMA specialization against cross-stock transformer generalization, which are different modeling strategies with different practical implications.

Periods of high market volatility are 'separately analyzed to assess model resilience,' but this post-hoc subgroup analysis is not a pre-specified primary comparison and does not control for the inflated performance on low-volatility periods in the main metric. A single model that performs well in calm markets and poorly in volatile ones will show good overall MAE without meeting the volatility robustness criterion.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random splitting of time series stock data is methodologically invalid — it inverts causality (training on future, testing on past), invalidates the ARIMA baseline (which requires consecutive temporal observations), and is the worst possible evaluation strategy for financial forecasting.

Technical indicators computed on the full time series before splitting (moving averages, RSI, MACD) embed look-ahead bias — the indicator at any historical date uses future prices in its computation window when calculated on the full dataset first.

MAE in price units is not comparable across stocks with different price levels; without normalization (MAPE, returns-based error), the aggregate MAE metric is dominated by high-priced stocks and meaningless for multi-stock comparison.

Per-stock ARIMA specialization vs. single-model transformer generalization is an asymmetric comparison of two different modeling philosophies — the design is testing specialization vs. generalization, not ARIMA vs. transformer architecture.

Volatility subgroup analysis is post-hoc and unspecified — a model with strong low-volatility performance and weak high-volatility performance can achieve good aggregate MAE, masking the failure mode the analysis claims to assess.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random splitting of time series stock data is methodologically invalid — it inverts causality (training on future, testing on past), invalidates the ARIMA baseline (which requires consecutive temporal observations), and is the worst possible evaluation strategy for financial forecasting.

Technical indicators computed on the full time series before splitting (moving averages, RSI, MACD) embed look-ahead bias — the indicator at any historical date uses future prices in its computation window when calculated on the full dataset first.

MAE in price units is not comparable across stocks with different price levels; without normalization (MAPE, returns-based error), the aggregate MAE metric is dominated by high-priced stocks and meaningless for multi-stock comparison.

Per-stock ARIMA specialization vs. single-model transformer generalization is an asymmetric comparison of two different modeling philosophies — the design is testing specialization vs. generalization, not ARIMA vs. transformer architecture.

Volatility subgroup analysis is post-hoc and unspecified — a model with strong low-volatility performance and weak high-volatility performance can achieve good aggregate MAE, masking the failure mode the analysis claims to assess.
